coredata.dc:title: A novel “turn-off” fluorescent sensor based on cranberry derived carbon dots to detect iron (III) and hypochlorite ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Şenol, Ayşe Merve
Onganer, Yavuz
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have been prepared for the quantitative determination of iron(III) (Fe3+) and hypochlorite (ClO−) ions separately. Firstly, CDs were successfully synthesized using a hydrothermal treatment which is a green and simple method from cranberry as a novel natural resource. They were precisely characterized using advanced instruments. Secondly, CDs were separately studied by absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy as a fluorescence sensor for various metal cations and anions, respectively. As a result of spectroscopic data, it was showed that CDs were effectively quenched with Fe3+ ions among various cations and ClO− ions among various anions, respectively. The fluorescence intensities of CDs were quenched by forming a non-radiative complex between Fe3+ and CDs, and LOD for Fe3+ was calculated as 0.75 nM in the range of 1.6 nM to 22.8 nM. Furthermore, their fluorescence intensities are quenched by oxidizing of the hydroxyl groups on surface of CDs with ClO− ions, which have strong oxidant properties, and a detection limit (LOD) for ClO− was calculated as 1.60 nM in the range of 9.9 nM to 78.0 nM. Also, the proposed method to detect Fe3+ and ClO− ions was successfully applied in real natural spring and tap water samples. All data showed that these novel CDs could be used as a highly selective and sensitive, and instantaneously responsive “turn-off” fluorescence sensor for Fe3+ and ClO− ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence sensor
Ferric ions
Hypochlorite ions
Cranberry

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorimetric determination of Fe3+ and ClO−
Fe3+ and ClO− sensing of CDs
Determination of Fe3+ and ClO− in the real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research has been supported by Ataturk University, Scientific Research Projects Coordination Unit (Grant Number: FAD-2019-7025). We thank Solomon Bezabeh Kassa for the one or two absorption measurements.

This research has been supported by Ataturk University, Scientific Research Projects Coordination Unit (Grant Number: FAD-2019-7025). We thank Solomon Bezabeh Kassa for the one or two absorption measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis. Absorption spectrum, (b) fluorescence spectrum (λexc = 340 nm) of CDs, (c) normalized fluorescence spectra, and (d) the fluorescence intensity of the CDs at various excitation wavelengths in the range of 330 nm to 450 nm with 10 nm increment.

The fluorescence spectra of CDs (a) in the absence and presence of various metal ions (0.625 mM), (b) including Fe3+ various concentrations in the range of 0.0016 µM to 620 µM. (c) Plot of F0/F versus Fe3+ various concentrations in the range of 0.0016 µM to 0.0228 µM. (λexc = 340 nm).
Histograms of F/F0, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the CDs toward the various competing metal ions (Mn+) in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions. (λexc = 340 nm).

The fluorescence spectra of CDs (a) in the absence and presence of 0.164 mM anions, (b) including ClO− various concentrations in the range of 0.0033 µM to 465 µM, (c) Plot of F0/F versus ClO− various concentrations in the range of 0.0099 µM to 0.078 µM. (λexc = 340 nm).
Histograms of F/F0, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the CDs toward the various competing anions (Mn−) in the absence and presence of ClO− ions. (λexc = 340 nm).
Determination of Fe3+ and ClO− ions in the various water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A novel “turn-off” fluorescent sensor based on cranberry derived carbon dots to detect iron (III) and hypochlorite ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Program of Medical Laboratory Techniques, Department of Medical Services and Techniques, University of Health Sciences, Istanbul 34668, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Medical Services and Techniques

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Program of Medical Laboratory Techniques, Department of Medical Services and Techniques, University of Health Sciences, Istanbul 34668, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Atatürk University, Erzurum 25240, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Atatürk University, Erzurum 25240, Turkey

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Program of Medical Laboratory Techniques, Department of Medical Services and Techniques, University of Health Sciences, Istanbul 34668, Turkey.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Medical Services and Techniques

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have been prepared for the quantitative determination of iron(III) (Fe3+) and hypochlorite (ClO−) ions separately. Firstly, CDs were successfully synthesized using a hydrothermal treatment which is a green and simple method from cranberry as a novel natural resource. They were precisely characterized using advanced instruments. Secondly, CDs were separately studied by absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy as a fluorescence sensor for various metal cations and anions, respectively. As a result of spectroscopic data, it was showed that CDs were effectively quenched with Fe3+ ions among various cations and ClO− ions among various anions, respectively. The fluorescence intensities of CDs were quenched by forming a non-radiative complex between Fe3+ and CDs, and LOD for Fe3+ was calculated as 0.75 nM in the range of 1.6 nM to 22.8 nM. Furthermore, their fluorescence intensities are quenched by oxidizing of the hydroxyl groups on surface of CDs with ClO− ions, which have strong oxidant properties, and a detection limit (LOD) for ClO− was calculated as 1.60 nM in the range of 9.9 nM to 78.0 nM. Also, the proposed method to detect Fe3+ and ClO− ions was successfully applied in real natural spring and tap water samples. All data showed that these novel CDs could be used as a highly selective and sensitive, and instantaneously responsive “turn-off” fluorescence sensor for Fe3+ and ClO− ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Fe3+ and ClO− ions play a crucial role in many biological and environmental processes. Fe3+ functions in coordination with different proteins in our body [1,2]. An abnormal level of Fe3+ in our body causes to various diseases. Fe3+ deficiency leads to various functional disorders in the lungs, liver, heart, and kidneys as well as anemia and diabetes while Fe3+ overloading induces to critical diseases as neurological function diseases and various cancers [3,4]. ClO−, which is a kind of the reactive oxygen species (ROS), is used in many fields as a purifier in sewage, a bleach in the paper and textile industries, and a disinfectant in drinking water [5,6]. Hypochlorous acid/hypochlorite (HOCl/ClO−) used in the killing of pathogens in drinking water plays a critical role in biological processes that affect human health [7]. Excess HClO/ClO− could lead to arteriosclerosis, neuronal and tissue deformation, inflammatory, kidney and cardiovascular diseases, and even cancer while inadequate HClO/ClO− could induce to many diseases because the pathogenic bacteria and viruses do not totally kill [8,9]. Up to now, many methods such as liquid chromatography/inductively coupled plasma–mass spectrometry [10,11], fluorescence [12–14], and electrochemical [15,16] methods have been employed to determine only one of these ions.

Fluorescence sensors compared to other methods have attracted great attention to researchers because of their many remarkable superiorities including rapidity, easily applicability, cheapness, precision and selectivity [12,17]. There are many studies on the fluorescence sensor potential of CDs. However, the studies, which are aimed to detect different ions with a single fluorescent probe, are limited [3,7,17–24]. CDs as compared with conventional fluorescent semiconductor nanoparticles and organic small molecules have quite noteworthy optical/chemical properties such as tunable emission, chemical/fluorescence stability, low-cytotoxicity, aqueous homogeneous solubility, and simple modification [12,25–27]. CDs are successfully applied in substantial areas such as fluorescent/electrochemical sensor [28], bio-imaging[29], photocatalysis [30], optoelectronic devices [31], antimicrobial activity [32], and drug delivery [33]. In recent years, the number of CDs synthesized using natural precursors is quite high. Their optical and structural properties (quantum efficiency, surface functional groups, the wavelength of maximum fluorescence, etc.) change to the content of natural precursors. As a consequence of these changing properties, the selectivity and sensitivity of CDs as a fluorescence probe have potentially changed. Therefore, the prepared CDs from natural precursors due to their important advantages such as easy access, low cost and simplicity are promising for fluorescent sensor studies [1,34,35]. Also, the use of CDs for detection of the anion and cation is very important for both environment and human health [5,26]. To date, natural source-derived CDs are frequently used to detect Fe3+ as a fluorescence sensor [24,36]. For example; Pseudo-stem of banana plant [37], Biomass waste [38], Rose-heart radish [39], Seville orange [40] derived CDs, etc. Natural source-derived CDs used to detect ClO− according to Fe3+ are limited. These carbon dots, which were examined to detect ClO− as a fluorescence sensor in the literature, were prepared from shaddock peel [20], kitchen-derived waste tea residue [7], chitin [41]. However, CDs have not seen to be studied as a fluorescence sensor to detect both Fe3+ and ClO− in the literature. In this study, cranberry as a novel natural precursor was used to prepare the CDs. Cranberry is a fruit that is rich in carbon sources including glycosides, flavones, and terpenes [42].

Herein, we report new functional fluorescent CDs for detection of Fe3+ and ClO− separately in water. Firstly, CDs in water were successfully synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal method from cranberry. The characterization of as-synthesized CDs was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and UV–Vis. absorption and steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy. Secondly, CDs were potentially studied as a fluorescence sensor for metal cations and anions, respectively. CDs were determined to be effectively quenched with Fe3+ and ClO− ions separately. LOD values were calculated for both ions. Then, their interference studies were performed for Fe3+ and ClO− ions, respectively. Thus, the CDs were found to have high accuracy and selectivity as well as a very fast response for the detection of Fe3+ and ClO− in very low LOD values. The proposed method was successfully applied in real water samples for each ion. This study could be a good alternative to CD’s fluorescence sensor studies.

Cranberries used for preparing CDs were picked up from Uzundere in Erzurum. KCl, LiCl, BaCl2·2H2O, CaCl2·2H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, ZnCl2, CoCl2·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, AlCl3, FeSO4·7H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, CrCl2, CrCl3·6H2O, HgCl2, CdCl2, AgNO3, and FeCl3 used for fluorescence sensor of Fe3+ ion detection were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. NaF, NaCl, NaBr, NaI, Na2CO3·H2O, Na2SO4, NaNO3, Na3PO4, Na2S, NaSCN, NaCN, CH3COONa, Na2C2O4 powders and NaClO solution used for fluorescence sensor of ClO− ion detection were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich.

20 g of cranberry were crumbled using liquid nitrogen. The crumbly cranberries were added to 100 mL water and sonicated for 10 min to completely disperse in water. The prepared extract was placed in an autoclave and kept at a constant temperature of 130 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the resulting mixture was separated from the larger particles using a filter membrane (0.22 µm). Next, it centrifuged at 13500 rpm for 30 min at 4 °C. Therefore, the final CDs were synthesized. The stock solution of CDs (0.015 g/mL) was prepared by adding distilled water.

TEM images were taken in a Hitachi HighTech HT7700 at 120 kV. XPS was explored by Spect‐Flex including a standard Al X‐ray source. XRD analysis of the sample was performed by a PANalytical’s X-ray diffractometers. FT-IR spectrum was collected by a Bruker Alpha ATR spectrometer, in the wavenumbers ranging from 650 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. All fluorescence and absorption spectra were taken by a Shimadzu RF-5301PC Spectrofluorophotometer and a Perkin Elmer Lambda 35 UV–Vis. Spectrophotometer, respectively. Photostability experiments were carried out with a xenon lamp on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu RF-5301PC). Zeta potential measurements and DLS analysis of the samples were made by a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZSP.

Relative fluorescence quantum yields (Φf) of the samples using the Parker-Rees method calculated as compared with quinine sulfate (Φf = 0.546 in 0.05 M H2SO4 as a reference) dye. Determination of Φf with this method has been successfully applied in previous studies [43,44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorimetric determination of Fe3+ and ClO−
ce:section.ce:para: CDs in pure water used for Fe3+ and ClO− ion detection were prepared by diluting approximately 90 times of the stock solution. Fistly, the cations were separately added into as-prepared CDs of 3.0 mL, respectively. The solutions were effectively mixed and were instantly taken into their fluorescence spectra. Then, the same procedure was repeated for the anions. In the experiments of real water samples, the same procedure was repeated using real water samples instead of pure water. In all fluorescence sensor measurements, the pH of the CD aqueous solution was 5.95.

The CDs were easily synthesized from cranberry at 130 °C in an autoclave. Firstly, they were structurally and morphologically characterized. In Fig. S1a and S1b, TEM image and histogram of particle size distribution were shown clearly that CDs with spherical shapes have sizes varying from 4.0 to 6.5 nm. Their particle size was calculated to be nearly 5.0 nm. Secondly, the surface functional groups of CDs were determined using FT-IR (Fig. S1c). As seen in Fig. 1 c, the broad band at 3318 cm−1 and the sharp band at 2921 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibrations of O–H and C–H groups, respectively. The bands at 1615 and 1512 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibrations of C = O and C = C. Moreover, the band around 1443 cm−1 and 1320 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of C-N and C-O-C. Additionally, the band at 1032 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of C-O. These groups provide that CDs are water-soluble and stable. [1,44–46]. The corresponding XRD diffractogram (in Fig. S1d) reflects a maximum peak of 2θ = 21.6°, which is confirmed to be randomly organized amorphous carbon of the CDs [7,45]. Furthermore, XPS was used to determine its surface chemical composition of CDs. In Fig S2a, the peaks at 531.1, 400.0, and 284.6 eV are confirmed that the CDs are consist of oxygen, nitrogen, and carbon, respectively. The deconvolution of O1s, N1s and C1s, which show the bonds between carbon, oxygen and nitrogen are given Fig. S2b, S2c and S2d, respectively. These peaks and their corresponding functional groups are observed as follows: 532.4 eV (C-OH/C-O-C) and 530.7 eV (C = O) for O1s spectrum, 401.3 (C-NH2) and 399.6 eV (C-N-C) for N1s spectrum, and at 284.4 eV (C–C/C = C), 285.8 eV (C-N) and 287.9 eV (C = O) for C1s spectrum [40,47,48]. The consistent results show that the CDs were prepared successfully.

Optical characterization of the CDs were evaluated using the absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques. The absorption of CDs has demonstrated a peak at approximately 283 nm, which is associated to π– π* transition between aromatic C=C bonds (Fig. 1a). The CDs exhibited a powerful fluorescence band (λmax) at 431 nm under the excitation wavelength (λex) of 340 nm (Fig. 1b). Since their fluorescence properties changed depending on the excitation wavelength of CDs, their fluorescence band maxima (λmax) and intensities were investigated by varying λex from 330 to 450 nm at 10 nm intervals. The normalized fluorescence spectra and fluorescence intensity of the CDs at various λex are given in Fig. 1c and 1d, respectively. The λmax of CDs shifted from 426 nm to 517 nm when λex varied from 330 nm to 450 nm (Fig. 1c). The wavelength at which CDs showed maximum fluorescence intensity was determined to be 340 nm (Fig. 1d). This is due to the differences in surface defects, particle sizes, and surface functional groups of the particles. Also, the Φf was calculated by using quinine sulfate as a reference and was found as 14.5%. The CDs have a quite high Φf in comparison with CDs prepared using natural initiators in the literature [1,40].

The absorption and fluorescence stability of as-prepared CDs were studied by the parameters of pH, temperature and ionic strength of the medium. As shown in Fig. S3a and S3b, absorption spectra of CDs in various pH solutions appeared no significant changes, whereas their fluorescence spectra varied. The fluorescence intensity of CDs increased when pH increased from 2 to 8, whereas it decreased when pH increased from 8 to 12 upon excitation at 340 nm. In addition, their fluorescence quantum yield values in various pH were calculated (Table S1. According to Table S1, CDs at pH 8 were determined to have the highest fluorescent quantum yield value as compatible with Fig. S3b. The all data showed that CDs are very stable in a wide pH range (pH 4–10). The effect of temperature in the range of 3 °C to 60 °C on the spectroscopic properties of CDs is also given in Fig. S4a and S4b. It was determined that their absorption and fluorescence intensity decreased due to increasing temperature [12]. Also, the effect of ionic strength on absorbance and fluorescence properties of CDs were studied in various concentration of NaCl (Fig. S5a and S5b). It was determined that the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased slightly until the 0.8 M concentration with increasing salt concentration and then remained constant at higher concentrations. Furthermore, the fluorescence quantum yields of CDs were calculated at various temperature and NaCl concentration. These data are accordance with the spectroscopic data. Consequently, CDs in water are stable over a wide pH, temperature and ionic strength medium. Also, the effects of various storage duration at 4 °C and xenon lamp irradiation time on their fluorescence stability were investigated (Fig. S6). The prepared CD stock solution remained stable at 4 °C in the refrigerator for almost 240 days (Fig. S6a). It was observed that there was no significant change in different xenon lamp irradiation time on CD’s fluorescence intensity (Fig. S6b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ and ClO− sensing of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the fluorescence responses of the CDs were investigated in the presence of 17 different metal cations to detect the selectivity of the CDs. In Fig. 2 a, it indicated that Fe3+ ions quenched the fluorescence intensity of CDs, while other cations caused little or no quenching effects (Fig. S7). This is stem from high affinity to functional groups carrying oxygen and nitrogen on their surface of CDs for Fe3+ in comparison with other cations. There is an electron transfer from CDs to Fe3+, and then fluorescence quenching takes place. It forms a non-radiative complex between Fe3+ and CDs [1]. Also, the selectivity of CDs was determined by the absorption studies (Fig. S8). The absorption intensity of CDs@Fe (Fe3+ loaded CDs) compared with CDs containing other metal cations in water was quite high. To understand the CDs quenching by Fe3+, UV–vis. absorption spectra of CDs, Fe3+ and CDs@Fe were given in Fig. S9. As seen in Fig. S9, there is no new band formation when Fe3+ is added to CD in water. Thus, it was determined that the fluorescence quenching was not caused by the formation of a new substance [29]. In addition, the zeta potentials of CD and CDs@Fe (CD including 0.332 µM Fe3+) were measured as −5.83 mV and −3.83 mV, respectively. This indicates that the CD surface interacts with the positively charged Fe3+ ions. Also, the fluorescence quantum yields of CDs were calculated by adding various metal cations to CDs (Table S2). All results are accordance with each other, showing fluorescence quenching between CDs and Fe3+.

The CDs are potentially used as a fluorescence sensor for the determination of Fe3+. The fluorescence spectra of the CDs were recorded in the range of 0.0016 µM-620 µM Fe3+. As presented in Fig. 2b, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was totally quenched when the Fe3+ ion concentration reached to 620 µM. The plots of F/Fo vs. [Fe3+] are non-linear in this concentration range (Fig. S10). Furthermore, one can see the absorption spectra of these solutions in Fig. S11. When the Fe3+ concentration in CDs solutions is increased, the absorption intensity of these solutions is proportionally increased.

Secondly, the fluorescence responses of the CDs were explored in the presence of 14 different anions to detect their selectivity. It was shown that ClO− ions quenched the fluorescence intensity of CDs, while other anions caused little or no quenching effects (Fig. 4 a and Fig. S13). This can be related to having a very strong oxidant property of hypochlorite ions. Thus, fluorescence intensities of CDs were quenched by oxidizing hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs with hypochlorite ions [49]. Moreover, the ClO− selectivity of the CDs was investigated by the absorption studies (Fig. S14). It has appeared that the absorption intensity of CDs@ClO (ClO− loaded CDs) are lower than other anions loaded CDs [50]. To understand more clearly the CDs quenching with ClO−, UV–vis. absorption spectra of CDs, ClO− and CDs@ClO were given in Fig. S15. As seen in Fig. S15, there is no new band formation when ClO− is added to CD in water. Decreasing in the absorption intensity of CDs@ClO could be due to the electrostatic attraction between CD and ClO−, a strong oxidizing agent. In addition, the zeta potentials of CD and CDs@ClO (CD including 0.332 µM ClO−) were measured as −5.83 mV and −13,1 mV, respectively. This showed that the CDs surface envelops with the negatively charged ClO− ions. Also, the fluorescence quantum yields of CDs were calculated by adding various anions to CDs (Table S3).

Newly prepared CDs were applied for the detection of ClO− ions. The fluorescence spectra of the CDs were recorded in the range of 0.0033 µM-465 µM ClO−. When the ClO− ion concentration reached 465 µM, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was almost completely quenched (Fig. 4b). The plots of F/Fo vs. [ClO−] are non-linear in this concentration range (Fig. S16). Meanwhile, CDs@ClO (ClO− loaded CDs) including increasing amount of ClO− were taken in absorption spectra. When ClO− concentration in CD solution increased, absorption intensity of CD@ClO is proportionally showed to be decreased (Fig. S17). To determine the sensitivity of CDs to ClO− ions, fluorescence spectra of CDs containing various concentrations of ClO− (0.0099–0.0780 µM) were recorded. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs is parallelly decreased when ClO− ion concentration is increased (Fig. S18). Consequently, it has been determined that CDs are highly sensitive to ClO− ions. In Fig. 4c, the plot of F0/F vs. [ClO−] showed a good linearity (R2 = 0.99). KSV was calculated as 1.508 µM−1. LOD was determined in the same way as Fe3+ ions. LOD for ClO− ions was found to be 1.60 nM. Also, the selectivity of CDs for ClO− detection in the presence of competing anions is investigated. Each anion was separately added to the CDs in water, and then ClO− was added to each of these systems. The fluorescence spectra of as-prepared solutions before and after ClO− addition were separately recorded. The conclusions indicated that any of the interfering thirteen anions did not prevent the determination of ClO− ions (Fig. 5).

Also, one of the most important parameters for fluorescence sensors is the response time. The effect of fluorescence response time (30–600 sec.) on CDs@Fe and CDs@ClO systems was investigated (Fig. S19 and Fig S20, respectively). CDs as a fluorescence sensor were found to respond quite rapidly to the addition of both Fe3+ and ClO−. This response time is max. 60 sec. In all fluorescence sensor studies in this manuscript, fluorescence spectra were obtained by waiting for a max. 60 sec.(1 min.). Furthermore, LOD values for Fe3+ and ClO− were compared according to other reported literatures (Table S4). In Table S4, it is seen that the LOD values of cranberry-derived CDs are quite low to detect Fe3+ and ClO− when compared to other carbon-derived quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fe3+ and ClO− in the real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The potential of CDs to determine Fe3+ and ClO− ions was investigated in tap water and natural spring water. Firstly, three tap and natural spring water samples containing various concentrations of Fe3+ (50, 100, and 150 µM) were prepared, and then their fluorescence measurements were taken. The same procedure was tested for various different concentrations of ClO− (50, 100, and 150 nM). The calculated data are given in Table 1 . The results showed that recoveries ranged from 99 to 100.5%. Also, the relative standard deviations (RSD) are very low. Therefore, the novel CDs can be applied to separately Fe3+ and ClO− in real water samples.

In the present study, a novel fluorescent CDs was synthesized using a simple and hydrothermal treatment method in an autoclave from cranberry as a natural resource. The CDs were successfully characterized by using advanced spectroscopic techniques. TEM images showed that the spherical CDs are about 5 nm. The results of XPS, XRD, and FTIR are compatible with each other. They prove that the structure and surface functional groups of CDs are consist of C, N, and O. Furthermore, the optical properties of the CDs were determined in different pH buffer solutions, temperatures and ionic strength media. CDs were proved to be a sensitive and selective “turn-off” fluorescent sensor for the detection of Fe3+ and ClO− with the LOD of 0.75 nM and 1.60 nM, respectively. Also, it was determined that these sensor systems can be easily applied to real water samples. In summary, this novel fluorescent sensor to detect each ion (Fe+3 and ClO−) suggests a quite promising and unique alternative because of the many advantages including low cost, very rapidity, easy applicability, high sensitivity and selectivity.

Ayşe Merve Şenol: Data curation, Visualization, Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Yavuz Onganer: Resources, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research has been supported by Ataturk University, Scientific Research Projects Coordination Unit (Grant Number: FAD-2019-7025). We thank Solomon Bezabeh Kassa for the one or two absorption measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2021.113655.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High quantum yield nitrogen-doped carbon dots: green synthesis and application as “off-on” fluorescent sensors for the determination of Fe3+ and adenosine triphosphate in biological samples
Sens. Actuat., B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Huang, Q. Li, Y. Chen, L. Tong, X. Lin, J. Zhu, Q. Tong, High quantum yield nitrogen-doped carbon dots: green synthesis and application as “off-on” fluorescent sensors for the determination of Fe3+ and adenosine triphosphate in biological samples, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 276 (2018) 82-88.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-functional fluorescent sensors based on CaMoO4:Eu3+ for detection of Iron(III) and dichromate ions in aqueous
Opt. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Guo, S. Song, X. Wang, X. Jiang, W. Lv, X. Yu, Y. Han, L. Wang, Dual-functional fluorescent sensors based on CaMoO4:Eu3+ for detection of Iron(III) and dichromate ions in aqueous, Optical Materials, 96 (2019) 109342.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An unusual “off-on” fluorescence sensor for iron(III) detection based on fluorescein–reduced graphene oxide functionalized with polyethyleneimine
Sens. Actuators, B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.M. Şenol, Y. Onganer, K. Meral, An unusual “off-on” fluorescence sensor for iron(III) detection based on fluorescein–reduced graphene oxide functionalized with polyethyleneimine, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 239 (2017) 343-351.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A highly selective and instantaneously responsive Schiff base fluorescent sensor for the “turn-off” detection of iron(iii), iron(ii), and copper(ii) ions
Anal. Methods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhu, Y. Duan, P. Li, H. Fan, T. Han, X. Huang, A highly selective and instantaneously responsive Schiff base fluorescent sensor for the “turn-off” detection of iron(iii), iron(ii), and copper(ii) ions, Analytical Methods, 11 (2019) 642-647.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 4-aminoantipyrine modified carbon dots and their analytical applications through response surface methodology
Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Yan, Y. Jiang, X. Sun, T. Ma, L. Chen, L. Chen, 4-aminoantipyrine modified carbon dots and their analytical applications through response surface methodology, Spectrochim Acta A Mol Biomol Spectrosc, 227 (2020) 117543.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile photoelectrochemical sensor for high sensitive ROS and AA detection based on graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets
Biosens. Bioelectron.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sustainable carbon nanodots synthesised from kitchen derived waste tea residue for highly selective fluorimetric recognition of free chlorine in acidic water: A waste utilization approach
J. Taiwan Inst. Chem. Eng.
